---
title: "Visualization Design Report"
---

This report documents the design decisions for the Epstein Files network visualization
product. Design choices derive from the research question ([Project Charta](project_charta.qmd))
and the data characteristics described in the [Data Report](data_report.qmd).

---

## Design Overview

The visualization is a **force-directed network graph** rendered in the browser using
WebGL. The central design goal is to let a user answer the research question — *who bridges
otherwise disconnected clusters?* — through direct, exploratory interaction with the network.

A dark theme on a near-black background (`#0D1117`) was chosen because it maximizes the
visual contrast of coloured nodes and weighted edges against the canvas, and because dark
themes make structural holes (negative space between clusters) visible at a glance. Light
backgrounds tend to wash out the gaps that are central to the structural-holes concept.

---

## Data-to-Visual Mapping

: Data-to-visual encoding table for the network graph. {#tbl-encoding-overview}

| Data dimension | Visual variable | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Betweenness centrality | Node size | Large nodes are bridge actors — the primary analytical signal |
| Community / domain cluster | Node colour (Louvain) | Colour pre-attentively separates clusters; community = domain proxy |
| Co-occurrence edge weight | Edge width | Stronger co-occurrence = thicker line; weak links fade visually |
| Entity type (PERSON vs ORG) | Detail panel label | Type is surfaced in the sidebar entity detail rather than a visual channel |
| Node label | Shown for top 25 by betweenness when zoomed in, or for the selected node | Avoids label overload at the overview zoom level |

---

## Layout

- **Canvas:** fullscreen (100 vw × 100 vh), no margins — the network fills the view.
- **Floating sidebar** (left, width ~320 px, dark glass panel): always visible; holds
  entity search, community filter chips, minimum-connections slider, and top structural
  bridges. Clicking a node opens a detailed panel with betweenness rank, co-occurrence
  count, and links to source documents (DOJ URLs from `online_url` field).
- **Minimal navbar** (top centre): project name pill only — navigation lives in the sidebar.
- **Legend overlay** (bottom right): collapsible three-row key — node size = influence,
  colour = community, edge thickness = co-occurrence.
- Controls overlay the canvas rather than displacing it — the graph always occupies the
  full viewport.

---

## Interaction Design

| Interaction | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| Click node | Open sidebar with entity details + list of source document links; camera pans to node |
| Hover node | White ring on nearest node; node + its direct neighbours are brightened, rest dimmed |
| Community filter chips | Isolate nodes belonging to selected community colours |
| Search bar | Highlight matching node; pan/zoom to it; node + neighbours brightened |
| Ego-network toggle | Collapse view to clicked node + its immediate neighbours |
| Guided story | Step through 6 pre-authored narrative findings; each step focuses a key actor |
| Zoom / pan | Mouse wheel + drag; zoom-to-fit and ± buttons in the right-centre controls overlay |

All source document links open the DOJ-hosted document in a new tab, providing full
provenance traceability for every displayed connection. This is a deliberate design choice
given the sensitivity of the subject matter.

---

## Technology Stack

| Component | Technology |
|---|---|
| Build tool | Vite |
| UI framework | React 18 |
| Component library | shadcn/ui (Tailwind CSS) |
| Network renderer | Cosmograph (WebGL, GPU-accelerated) |
| State management | Zustand |
| Data format | `nodes.json` + `edges.json` (generated by the EDA pipeline) |

**Why Cosmograph?** Standard SVG-based force layouts (e.g. D3) struggle above ~5 000
nodes in a browser. Cosmograph's WebGL renderer handles 100 000+ nodes at interactive
frame rates. For the current sample (~1 000 docs) D3 would suffice, but Cosmograph
future-proofs the visualization for a larger sample or the full corpus.

**Why React + shadcn/ui?** The sidebar and filter controls require reactive state
(selected node, active filters). shadcn/ui provides accessible, unstyled components that
integrate cleanly with Tailwind's dark-mode utilities without imposing a heavy design
system.

---

## Prototype

The current prototype is deployed at **[data.kulakci.de](https://data.kulakci.de)** via
GitHub Pages (auto-deploys on push to `main`).

> Note: The project charta references an earlier prototype concept using Streamlit.
> Following team review, the stack was updated to React + Cosmograph for performance
> reasons. The analytical outputs (network JSON files) are identical regardless of
> frontend technology.

---

## Design Rationale

The core decision is to make **betweenness centrality the primary visual signal** (node
size). This directly encodes the answer to the research question in the most salient
visual channel — users can identify bridge actors before reading a single label.

An alternative was using node colour for betweenness (heatmap) and size for degree. This
was rejected because community membership is categorical and colour is the pre-attentive
variable best suited to categorical distinctions, while betweenness varies continuously
and is better encoded in a magnitude channel (size).

Provenance links ensure every displayed connection is traceable to a specific document.
This supports scientific credibility and guards against misleading inference from noisy
entity extraction.
